data.title: Russia prepares for return of foreign companies, but conditions apply
data.description:
Russia is working on a legal framework for foreign companies to re-enter the country, amid hopes that Western sanctions will eventually be lifted. But many companies are wary of making concrete plans to return. 

"They're all doing due diligence," Chris Weafer, who runs business consultancy Macro Advisory, told Bloomberg. But most companies wouldn't consider returning till there is a permanent peace deal, as "nobody wants to come back too soon and then have to exit again," Weafer added. 

Russia's finance ministry has been drafting conditions for foreign companies seeking to return, which reportedly include requirements for localizing some production and agreeing to technology transfers. Russian businesses would also be given a say on companies returning to certain sectors.

Many companies sold their Russian subsidiaries after Moscow's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022. The deals included buyback clauses, which would enable them to reclaim the assets at a cheaper price, although this is unlikely to happen.

"All new investors who want to come to Russia will receive the most favorable working conditions," Anatoly Aksakov, head of the financial markets committee in Russia's lower house of parliament, told Bloomberg. 

But companies will have to pay full market prices to reclaim the assets they sold when they decided to exit the country, Aksakov added.

Italian heating systems maker Ariston is set to restart operations in Russia after it was given back control of its local unit. The subsidiary had been seized and placed under the temporary control of Russia's Gazprom. 

South Korea's LG Electronics recently restarted its operations at its plant near Moscow. Hyundai (OTCPK:HYMTF) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) are assessing the feasibility of operating in a post-war Russian market, The Moscow Times reported last month. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Regions Financial Non-GAAP EPS of $0.54 beats by $0.03, revenue of $1.78B misses by $50M
data.description:
• Regions Financial press release (NYSE:RF): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.54 beats by $0.03.
  • Revenue of $1.78B (+1.7% Y/Y) misses by $50M.
  • Adjusted total revenue of $1.81B.

data.title: Texas Capital Bancshares Non-GAAP EPS of $0.92 misses by $0.03, revenue of $280.48M misses by $2.82M
data.description:
• Texas Capital Bancshares press release (NASDAQ:TCBI): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.92 misses by $0.03.
  • Revenue of $280.48M (+9.4% Y/Y) misses by $2.82M.
  • Capital ratios continue to be strong, including 11.6% CET1 and 15.6% Total Capital
  • Provision for credit losses for the first quarter of 2025 was $17.0 million, compared to $18.0 million for the fourth quarter of 2024. The $17.0 million provision for credit losses recorded in the first quarter of 2025 resulted primarily from an increase in criticized loans and $9.8 million in net charge-offs, as well as uncertainty in the economic outlook.

data.title: UnitedHealth Non-GAAP EPS of $7.20 misses by $0.09, revenue of $109.58B misses by $2.02B
data.description:
• UnitedHealth press release (NYSE:UNH): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $7.20 misses by $0.09.
  • Revenue of $109.58B (+9.8% Y/Y) misses by $2.02B.
  • Consumers Served by UnitedHealthcare Increased by 780,000 Year to Date
  • Optum Health Continues to Expect to Serve 650,000 New Value-Based Care Patients in 2025
  • The first quarter medical care ratio was 84.8% compared to 84.3% in 2024. 
  • Days claims payable of 45.5 compared to 47.0 in the fourth quarter 2024 and 47.1 in first quarter 2024. This was driven by the Medicare Part D program changes noted previously.
  • The first quarter 2025 operating cost ratio of 12.4% compared to 14.1% in 2024, reflecting revenue impacts from the Part D program and increased technological and other operating efficiencies across UnitedHealthcare and Optum.
  • Revised 2025 Earnings Outlook to $24.65 to $25.15 Per Share , Adjusted Earnings $26 to $26.50 Per Share vs $29.74 consensus

data.title: Nvidia chief in Beijing after U.S. restricts AI chip exports
Biggest stock movers Thursday: TSM, AA, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged higher in Thursday’s premarket session amid renewed optimism over potential negotiated settlements between the U.S. and key trading partners on tariff-related disputes.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Taiwan Semiconductor (NYSE:TSM) +5% – Shares advanced after the chipmaker posted mixed Q1 results, with profit surging 60% Y/Y, surpassing expectations on strong demand for AI chips. The company issued upbeat guidance, forecasting Q2 revenue between $28.4B and $29.2B, above the $27.22B consensus. For 2025, TSMC expects revenue growth in the mid-20s% range in U.S. dollar terms. Looking further ahead, management projects a revenue CAGR of nearly 20% from 2024 to 2029, a long-term gross margin of 53% or higher, and a return on equity above 25% through the cycle.
  • MongoDB (NASDAQ:MDB) +2.5% – Shares edged higher after Redburn upgraded the database software provider to Neutral from Sell, citing limited downside risk. Analyst Alexander Haissl noted that MongoDB’s valuation multiple is near all-time lows and earnings expectations have already been reset. While macro-related risks persist, Haissl said they are not specific to the company. The firm set a price target of $170, implying a 6.2% upside from the last close.

Biggest stock losers

  • Alcoa (NYSE:AA) -3% – Shares slipped after the company posted mixed Q1 results and warned that tariff-related costs are set to rise sharply. Alcoa expects to incur $90M in costs this quarter from U.S. tariffs on aluminum imports from Canada, up from $20M in Q1. CEO William Oplinger noted that about 70% of the company’s Canadian aluminum is shipped to the U.S. and now faces a 25% tariff, weighing on profits by an estimated $100M annually. He added that steep U.S. tariffs on Chinese inputs could further increase annual costs by $10M–$15M due to limited alternative suppliers. While higher domestic aluminum prices may provide some relief, Oplinger said ramping up U.S. production remains impractical in the short term, undercutting a key goal of the Trump administration's tariff policy.

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow futures rise after tech led selloff
data.description:
Stock index futures were higher on Thursday, while yields gained, as Wall Street hoped for a rebound a day after a tech-driven selloff.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) +0.8%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) +1%, and Dow futures (INDU) +0.9%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 3 basis points to 4.32%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 3 basis points to 3.82%.

Wall Street on Wednesday ended deep in the red after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell expressed concerns about the likely higher-than-anticipated effects from tariffs, including possibly putting pressure on the central bank's dual mandate of stable prices and strong employment.

"Ahead of the Easter weekend, risk assets sold off again, as ongoing trade tensions and hawkish comments from Fed Chair Powell led to growing concern about the near-term outlook, particularly if the Fed would be reluctant to ease policy," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

"In terms of the latest on the trade front, investors were most concerned about chipmakers yesterday, which followed the new restrictions from the Donald Trump administration on semiconductor exports to China," Allen said.

On the economic front, eyes will be on the March housing starts and permits, initial jobless claims, and the April Philly Fed business outlook, all landing at the same time.

The initial jobless claims are expected to rise to 225K, and the Philly Fed business outlook is anticipated to come in at  2.2.

"The US Philly Fed business sentiment survey is due. All sentiment data is extremely suspect nowadays, but markets are focused on the corporate inactivity amidst policy uncertainty. Even unreliable evidence, therefore, has influence," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

Markets will be closed tomorrow on account of Good Friday.

data.title: Goldman Sachs warns of potential $800B U.S. selloff in Chinese stocks
data.description:
Goldman Sachs has issued a stark warning about the financial risks posed by growing tensions between the U.S. and China. In an extreme scenario of financial decoupling, U.S. investors could be forced to divest as much as $800 billion worth of Chinese equities, the investment bank estimates.

Currently, about 7% of the market capitalization of Chinese firms' American Depositary Receipts (ADRs) is held by U.S. institutions. These investors may face limitations in trading Hong Kong-listed shares, particularly if major companies like Alibaba face involuntary delisting from U.S. exchanges, Goldman analysts noted.

The bank's analysis adds to mounting concerns from global financial institutions as the possibility of a U.S.-China financial split becomes more tangible amid escalating trade and regulatory friction.

China ETFs: (NYSEARCA:KWEB), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:FXI), (ASHR), (YIN), (YANG), (GXC), (FXP), (KBA), (CXSE), (CNYA), (CHAU), (FLCH), (YXI), (FCA), (KALL), (JCHI)

The trade war between the world’s two largest economies continues to weigh on global markets and economic growth. Earlier this month, Trump escalated a tense trade war with China by raising tariffs to a cumulative 145%, prompting a swift retaliation from Beijing with a 125% duty on U.S. goods.

The offshore yuan (CNY:USD) fell nearly 0.2% against the dollar at the time of writing. The Australian dollar (AUD:USD), a China-proxy currency, was also down 0.24%. S&P 500 (SPX) Index futures were up 0.83%.

US-listed Chinese stocks of relevance: Alibaba (BABA) +4.4% premarket, Baidu (BIDU) +2.69%, PDD (PDD) +1.79%, JD.com (JD) +1.61%, NetEase (NTES) +4.2%, Trip.com (TCOM) +3.76%.

data.title: Oil set for weekly gain, gold overtakes Mag 7 stocks as most crowded trade on Wall Street
data.description:
Oil prices climbed to a near two-week high on Thursday on supply concerns, while gold (XAUUSD:CUR) bumped the 'Magnificent Seven' tech stocks to take the top spot as a crowded trade, according to a BofA survey.

The United States imposed new sanctions to curb Iranian oil exports, while adding to supply concerns, the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries said on Wednesday it had received updated plans for Iraq, Kazakhstan, and other countries to make further output cuts to compensate for pumping above quotas.

West Texas Intermediate (CL1:COM) crude futures rose ~1% to $63.08/bbl, Brent crude (CO1:COM) up +0.8% to $66.38 - both set for a weekly gain. 

Inventory data in the US also provided some support. 

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

In other news, China’s apparent openness to talks with the US boosted sentiment in global gas markets. European gas and North Asia LNG prices both recorded strong gains, ANZ Research reported.

Gold prices, meanwhile, pulled back from record highs as the U.S. dollar edged higher. 

According to the latest Bank of America fund managers survey  published this week, nearly half of the fund managers surveyed (49%) see long gold, or bets that gold prices will rise, as the most crowded trade in the market right now. This marks the first time in two years that fund managers did not see the Magnificent Seven as Wall Street's most crowded trade, according to the survey.

 

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: QXO drops 7%, prices stock offering at $13.25 per share
